title: Career and Professional Development Services
summary: Career services can help earlier than most students think, whether you need a resume, a campus job, an internship plan, or just a starting point.

modes.label: In person
modes.summary: Use career services on campus for a resume review, internship planning, interview prep, or job search support.

steps.title: Choose the support goal before you book
steps.description: Career support is broader than one office visit, so first decide whether you need a resume review, job search help, internship planning, or interview prep.
bullets: Open the jobs and careers page.
Pick the support type that best matches your immediate need.
If needed, log into Handshake or the matching platform the page points you to.

visual.title: Resume help, internships, jobs, and planning
visual.caption: The first win is narrowing the reason you are going.

steps.title: Bring the materials you already have
steps.description: Career help works better when you bring your current draft, even if it feels rough or incomplete.
bullets: Bring your current resume or a list of jobs, clubs, or class projects.
Bring a job description if you are applying to something specific.
Write down what feels hardest: starting, wording, confidence, or knowing where to look.
steps.reassurance: You do not need a polished resume to deserve career help. A messy draft is exactly what these meetings are for.

visual.title: Any draft is better than a blank page
visual.caption: Career support gets better once there is something concrete to react to.

steps.title: Leave with one career step, not a vague feeling
steps.description: A useful meeting should end with one next action you can do this week.
bullets: Ask what one change matters most for your resume or plan.
Ask what platform or next meeting you should use after this.
Before you leave, decide whether your next move is revise, apply, or schedule another visit.

visual.title: One real move forward
visual.caption: You are trying to leave with direction, not with a perfect career plan.

modes.label: Online
modes.summary: Use the jobs and careers page plus Handshake or other ASU tools to get virtual career support.

steps.title: Open the right career platform
steps.description: ASU career support often routes through Handshake, appointment systems, or online resume review tools.
bullets: Start on the ASU jobs and careers page.
Follow the link to Handshake or the appointment platform you need.
Choose the virtual support type that fits your goal.

visual.title: Handshake and career appointment tools
visual.caption: The main trick is getting into the right platform first.

steps.title: Upload or open your current materials
steps.description: If the support is virtual, your file is the conversation starter.
bullets: Upload your resume, cover letter, or portfolio draft if asked.
Keep a job posting open if you are tailoring for something specific.
Join the appointment with your materials already on screen.
steps.reassurance: A rough draft is still a real starting point. It is normal to show up with something unfinished.

visual.title: Have the file open before you join
visual.caption: That makes the session feel more practical immediately.

steps.title: Turn feedback into action quickly
steps.description: Career advice is most useful when you apply it the same day while the suggestions are still fresh.
bullets: Update your draft soon after the session.
Save any recommended job boards, events, or next appointments.
Apply to one role or schedule one follow-up action this week.

visual.title: Feedback fades if it sits too long
visual.caption: A good next step is often one application or one cleaner draft.

modes.label: Drop-in
modes.summary: Use drop-in help for a quick resume review, one job-search question, or a fast sense check before applying.

steps.title: Bring one file or one question
steps.description: Drop-in career help works best when the visit is tightly focused.
bullets: Bring your resume draft or a single job posting.
Know whether you need feedback, search direction, or a quick review.
Be ready to tell them what you want to leave with.

visual.title: One draft or one question is enough
visual.caption: Drop-in is for getting moving, not solving every career question at once.

steps.title: Ask for the one most important fix
steps.description: A short visit is most useful when you ask for the highest-impact change rather than general feedback.
bullets: Ask what one change matters most before you apply.
Ask whether the resume is readable for the kind of role you want.
Ask where to go next if you need more than a quick review.
steps.reassurance: It is normal to want a quick signal that you are not completely off-track before applying anywhere.

visual.title: What is the biggest fix before I send this?
visual.caption: That question usually gets a sharper answer than “What do you think?”

steps.title: Book the longer support if your issue is bigger
steps.description: If the quick conversation surfaces bigger gaps, convert it into a longer appointment instead of trying to force it into drop-in time.
bullets: Ask whether a longer appointment would help more.
Save the follow-up booking link or office name.
Make the follow-up decision before you leave.

visual.title: Use quick help to get pointed in the right direction
visual.caption: Drop-in works best when it leads cleanly to the next support step.

questionSet.defaultOpener: Hi, I want to make sure I am using career support in the smartest way for where I am right now.
questionSet.defaultProTip: Bring the actual resume or job description if you have one. Career support gets sharper when there is something concrete on the screen.
questionSet.firstTimeIntro: A lot of students wait too long to use career services because they think they need a perfect plan first. You do not.

scenarioPlaceholders.class: I am trying to keep school on track, but I also need to think about jobs or internships and I do not know where to start.
scenarioPlaceholders.money: I need a job or internship plan that actually helps with money, not just vague career advice.
scenarioPlaceholders.overwhelmed: I know I should use career services, but the whole job and internship process feels overwhelming.
scenarioPlaceholders.schedule: I need help figuring out how career planning fits into this semester without becoming another huge task.
scenarioPlaceholders.something-else: I want career help, but I do not know what kind of appointment or question makes the most sense.

first-year: What kind of career support makes the most sense for a first-year student?
Should I be thinking about campus jobs, internships, or just a resume first?
What should I build first if I do not have much experience yet?
What is one step I can take this week that actually matters?
What do students usually wait too long to ask career services?
second-year: What should I be doing now to be more ready for internships or jobs later?
Is my resume focused on the right experiences yet?
What kind of appointment or resource would help me most next?
How should I use Handshake or campus career tools well?
What is one practical career step I should not push off this semester?
third-year: What should I prioritize right now: internship search, resume improvement, or interview prep?
Am I late on anything career-related, or am I still in a normal spot?
What is the most strategic next step for my field or interests?
How do I know whether my current materials are strong enough to apply?
What should I do after this meeting to keep momentum?
fourth-year-plus: What is the highest-impact career step for me right now?
How should I approach applications, interviews, or job strategy from this point?
What support is most useful if I feel behind on career planning late in college?
What should I fix first in my materials before applying seriously?
What is one question students at my stage usually do not think to ask?
